title: The trigger of being in a vehicle with someone else driving
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The passenger seat and the freeze response

You're sitting in the passenger seat. Your partner is driving. Or maybe a friend. Or a parent. You aren't in control of the vehicle, and something about that feels wrong in a way you can't quite name. Your chest tightens. Your jaw clenches. You start watching the road like your life depends on it, because some part of you believes it does.

I've been there. Honestly, I've spent years in that passenger seat, white-knuckling the door handle, my foot pressing an imaginary brake pedal through the floorboard. It's exhausting. And it's confusing, because the person driving is competent. They haven't crashed. They aren't drunk or reckless. So why does my nervous system scream like we're about to die?

Here's the thing: this trigger isn't about the driver. It's not about their skill or their safety record. It's about your history. It's about what happened to you when you were small and powerless, when someone else was in control of your safety and they failed you. The vehicle becomes a metaphor. The passenger seat becomes a trap.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stored in the body as incomplete survival responses. When you were a child, you might have wanted to run or fight or scream, but you couldn't. You froze instead. That freeze response didn't go away. It stayed in your tissues, waiting for a moment that felt even remotely similar to the original threat. And now, sitting in a moving car with someone else at the wheel, your body says: This is it. We're trapped again.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying every passenger seat trigger comes from childhood trauma. Sometimes it's a car accident you survived. Sometimes it's a near miss. But for many of us with complex trauma, the trigger is older and deeper. It's about the fundamental experience of having no control over your own safety while someone else makes decisions that affect your survival. That's not just a car thing. That's a life thing.

Right?

Why the nervous system treats driving like a threat assessment

Dan Siegel, the psychiatrist who coined the term "window of tolerance," explains that our nervous system has a range within which we can handle stress without flipping into fight, flight, or freeze. When you're triggered in a vehicle, you've likely slipped outside that window. Your prefrontal cortex, the thinking part of your brain, goes offline. Your limbic system takes over. You're no longer assessing the situation rationally. You're reacting to a perceived threat as if it's happening right now, even if the driver is doing just fine.

I've had moments where I'm gripping the seat so hard my fingers ache, and my brain is screaming we're going too fast or they're not looking or that was too close. And then I look at the speedometer and we're going the speed limit. The driver just checked their blind spot. The other car was a normal distance away. My perception is warped by my own history.

What makes this so hard is that the trigger is both physical and relational. You're not just reacting to the movement of the car. You're reacting to the fact that another person is making choices about your safety. And if your history includes caregivers who were unpredictable, neglectful, or abusive, your nervous system learned that other people cannot be trusted with your life. That learning doesn't just go away because you're an adult now. It stays encoded in your body.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic helped me understand that my passenger seat panic was not a character flaw. It was a trauma response. It was my system trying to keep me safe in a situation that felt, to my body, like a repeat of the past.

Think about it. Your body doesn't know the difference between a dangerous driver and a safe driver when the trigger is activated. It only knows the feeling of being in a moving container with someone else in control. That feeling is enough to launch you into survival mode. And survival mode is not a place where you can calmly ask someone to slow down or change lanes. Survival mode is where you dissociate, freeze, or snap at the driver for no apparent reason.

The relational layer: what the driver doesn't understand

This is where it gets messy, because the trigger isn't just about the car. It's about the relationship. If you're in a vehicle with a partner or a parent or a close friend, the dynamic of control and trust plays out in real time. And if your trauma history includes relational betrayal, that driver's smallest mistake can feel like a violation.

I remember riding with a friend who was a perfectly good driver. She took a turn a little wide, and I felt a surge of rage so intense I had to look away. The rage wasn't about the turn. It was about every time I was put in danger by someone who was supposed to protect me. The turn was just the match. The gasoline was already there.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist and author, writes about how trauma survivors often experience their triggers as if the past is happening in the present. The car becomes a time machine. You're not just in a Toyota with your sister. You're in the backseat of your childhood, helpless and scared, waiting for the crash that never came but always felt imminent.

This is why trying to "just relax" or "trust the driver" usually doesn't work. You can't logic your way out of a nervous system response. Your body has its own intelligence, and it's not interested in your rational arguments. It's interested in survival. And until your body learns that this situation is actually safe, you will keep reacting.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work explores how trauma can be passed down through generations, showing up in our bodies and reactions without any conscious memory of the original event. It helped me understand that my passenger seat panic might not even be entirely mine. It might be my mother's fear, or my grandmother's, carried in my cells.

Small steps for reclaiming the passenger seat

I'm not going to tell you that you can cure this trigger overnight. That's not how trauma works. But I have found some things that help, and I want to share them with you, not as a prescription, but as possibilities.

First, name it. Out loud if you can. Say to the driver, "I get anxious as a passenger sometimes. It's not about you. It's my nervous system." This does two things. It gives you back some agency by speaking your truth, and it lets the driver know that your reactions aren't a judgment of their driving. Most people are more understanding than we expect.

Second, orient yourself. Stephen Porges, the creator of Polyvagal Theory, talks about how our nervous system uses cues of safety from the environment to calm down. Look out the window. Notice the trees, the sky, the buildings. Feel the seat beneath you. Put your hand on your chest. Let your eyes move slowly, taking in your surroundings. This tells your brain: I am here, in this moment, not in the past.

Third, give your body something to do. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It extends your exhale naturally, which activates the vagus nerve and signals safety to your system. I keep one in my bag for car rides. It's small, discreet, and it gives me something to focus on besides the rising panic.

Fourth, reduce sensory overload. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower the volume of the road, the engine, the radio, without blocking out everything. For me, the sound of tires on pavement can be a trigger all by itself. These earplugs take the edge off.

And fifth, let yourself feel the feelings without judging them. You don't have to fix the trigger. You just have to survive it. And you will. You've survived worse. Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your shoulders drop? Did your breath catch? That's your system responding to being seen.

The open ending: you don't have to be a good passenger

I'm not going to wrap this up neatly, because there's no neat ending to living with trauma. Some days you'll get in the passenger seat and feel fine. Other days you'll cancel plans because you can't face it. Both are okay. You don't have to be a "good passenger" to be a good person. You don't have to push through every trigger to prove you're healed.

What matters is that you understand why this happens. That you stop blaming yourself for it. That you find small ways to support your nervous system, even when it feels like your body is betraying you. Because it's not betraying you. It's trying to protect you. It's just working with old information.

Wild, right? Your body is still fighting a war that ended years ago. But it doesn't have to fight alone. You can learn to be with it. You can learn to calm it, slowly, imperfectly, over time. And every time you survive a triggered car ride, your system learns something new. It learns that this time, you made it. This time, you're okay.

That's enough. That's real progress.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link)
  • Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link)
  • Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link)
